metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the `discovery.md` and `docs/user-guide.md` additions that `lens` is absent from `unavailable` entries in the JSON output, and instruct agents to use the metric `id`-to-lens mapping (or the metric id directly) rather than the `lens` field to identify unavailable code-health metrics.
evidence: src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py `_entry_for()`: unavailable branch returns `{'unavailable': {'reason': ..., 'accruing_since': ...}}` — no `lens` key
src/rebar/schemas/metrics.schema.json: `lens` is a property of the metric entry object but is NOT in `required` and absent from the unavailable sub-object

Plan Files section: 'cite the registered code-health-lens metric values (module-size distribution, oversized-module count..., churn, refactor-to-addition ratio, cap-change events)' — implies the agent can identify lens from JSON output, but unavailable entries for these same metrics won't carry `lens`
findings.finding: In the actual JSON output of `rebar metrics --output json`, `unavailable` entries do NOT carry a `lens` field — only value entries do (confirmed in `_entry_for()`: the unavailable branch returns `{"unavailable": {...}}` with no `lens` key). The plan instructs agents to cite metrics by lens (e.g., 'cite the code-health lens values'), but an agent parsing the JSON output cannot determine the lens of an unavailable metric from the output alone, creating a processing gap between the cited schema and the actual output structure.
findings.location: Files section — `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md` guidance; `docs/user-guide.md` Metrics section spec
scenarios: A discovery subagent iterates the JSON output filtering for `lens == 'code_health'` entries — unavailable code_health metrics are silently excluded because they lack the `lens` field, making the agent think no code_health metrics are registered
The user-guide Metrics section documents `source`/`confidence` alongside `lens` as always-present fields, but this is false for unavailable entries
findings.suggested_fix: In the user-guide Metrics section, note that value entries carry `{lens, source, confidence, value}` while unavailable entries carry only `{unavailable: {reason, accruing_since}}` — `lens` is absent from unavailable entries; agents should identify the lens by metric id using the documented id→lens table.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the `discovery.md` guidance, replace the hardcoded '800-line cap' reference with 'the project's current module-size cap (sourced from `.github/module-size-limit.txt` and reported in the `module_size_distribution` value)' so agents read the live cap rather than a potentially stale constant.
evidence: Plan text: 'module-size trend vs the 800 cap'
git_metrics.py line 77: `return int((Path(repo_root) / _LIMIT_FILE).read_text(encoding='utf-8').strip())` — cap is read from `.github/module-size-limit.txt`, not hardcoded
git_metrics.py line 35: `_LIMIT_FILE = '.github/module-size-limit.txt'`
AGENTS.md: 'hard cap 800' and 'changing it requires an administrator to override' — confirms the value is mutable via the file
findings.finding: The plan's Files section for `discovery.md` instructs the author to reference 'module-size trend against the locked 800-line cap,' but `git_metrics.py` reads the cap dynamically from `.github/module-size-limit.txt` at runtime — the cap is file-sourced and not hardcoded. If an executor writes discovery.md citing '800' as the fixed cap, agents reading it will have a stale/incorrect contract once the limit file changes (the plan even notes in AGENTS.md that the cap requires an admin override to change, confirming it IS mutable).
findings.location: Files section — `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md` description: 'module-size trend against the locked 800-line cap'
scenarios: An executor writes discovery.md citing 'the 800-line cap' as a fixed value; the limit file is later changed by an admin; the skill now misinforms agents about the actual threshold.
A consuming agent reads the skill and hardcodes 800 in its own analysis, missing that the real cap (read live by `rebar metrics`) may differ.
findings.suggested_fix: Write: 'the module-size trend against the project's current cap (read from `.github/module-size-limit.txt` and surfaced in the `module_size_distribution` metric value)' instead of 'the locked 800-line cap'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either update the grandparent epic (86ac) to include a discoverability/agent-skill track that covers `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md`, or create/reference a separate parent ticket authorising rebar-janitor skill updates before this leaf is reviewed — the current leaf delivers something outside any ancestor's declared scope.
evidence: Parent 9a5a file-impact list: src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py, src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py, src/rebar/_cli/help/metrics.txt, src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt, tests/golden/cli_help/*.out, docs/cli-reference.md, tests/interfaces/contracts/test_help_overview_coverage.py, tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py — no examples/ or agent-skill files listed.
Grandparent 86ac Decomposition: 'Three tracks; Command … Instrumentation … Backfill …' — no discoverability or agent-skill track.
Grandparent 86ac Success Criteria: none mention rebar-janitor or agent-skill consumers.
ABSENCE: 'examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor' appears nowhere in 9a5a or 86ac descriptions.
findings.finding: The leaf delivers an update to `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md` — teaching the rebar-janitor agent skill to consult `rebar metrics`. Neither the immediate parent (9a5a, the metrics CLI command ticket) nor the grandparent epic (86ac) mention rebar-janitor, agent-skill examples, or the `examples/` directory anywhere in their scope, file-impact lists, decomposition, success criteria, or acceptance criteria. This is a deliverable outside the parent chain's declared scope, not a consistent narrowing of it.
scenarios: An implementer following 9a5a's contract would not know to touch the rebar-janitor skill; if this leaf lands without the parent being updated, the parent's scope record remains inconsistent with what was actually delivered under the epic.
A future G7 check on 86ac's epic-rollup will see a delivered file (examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md) with no lineage in the epic's declared scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fourth track to 86ac's Decomposition: 'Discoverability: teach rebar-janitor to consult `rebar metrics` (examples/agent-skills/…) and add user-facing docs (docs/user-guide.md, docs/reuse-surface.md, AGENTS.md).' Then re-review this leaf against the updated parent. Alternatively, if the rebar-janitor update is the main point, scope it as a standalone sibling task under 86ac rather than under 9a5a.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete detection rule to the `discovery.md` guidance (e.g., 'check for `.rebar/.env-id` in the repo root; if present, this is a rebar store and `rebar metrics` is available') so the 'when the repo is a rebar store' branch condition is executable.
evidence: Plan Files section: 'add that a subagent should run `rebar metrics --since <date> --until <date> --output json` (when the repo is a rebar store)'
src/rebar/_commands/claim.py and _seam.py use `os.path.isfile(os.path.join(tracker, '.env-id'))` as the canonical store-presence test
No skill file (SKILL.md, discovery.md, phases/*.md) defines how to detect a rebar store — the condition is referenced but the detection rule is absent from the plan
findings.finding: The discovery.md instruction 'when the repo is a rebar store' references a condition that has no detection protocol defined anywhere in the skill files or the plan. The actual detection marker is `.rebar/.env-id` (used by claim, transition, and other commands as the canonical store-presence check), but the plan does not specify this, leaving the subagent with an undefined decision rule.
scenarios: A subagent running on a plain git repo (no .rebar/.env-id) invokes `rebar metrics` anyway and gets a confusing error or empty output, then treats absence as zero
A subagent skips the metrics step on a valid rebar store because it doesn't know what to check
findings.suggested_fix: In the discovery.md addition, replace 'when the repo is a rebar store' with 'when `.rebar/.env-id` exists in the repo root (the rebar store-presence marker)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten each AC's grep pattern to anchor on the required content (e.g. AC1: `grep -E 'rebar metrics --since'`; AC2: `grep -E '## Metrics'` plus `grep -c 'agent_process\|code_health\|delivery\|gate_economics'`; AC4: `grep -F 'rebar.metrics'`) so the assertions cannot pass on incidental prose mentions.
evidence: AC1: 'asserting the rebar metrics reference and the unavailable-state caveat are present' — no pattern given.
AC2: 'a grep/test for the section heading and the lens names' — no pattern given.
AC3: 'asserted by a grep/test' — no pattern given.
AC4: 'asserted by a grep/test for ... rebar.metrics' — lens name but no anchor.
VCDT #1: substring false-positive risk — grep 'rebar metrics' matches any prose mention, not just the instructional command form.
findings.finding: ACs 1–4 specify grep/test assertions but provide no grep pattern or anchor, creating a substring false-positive risk: a casual mention of 'rebar metrics' in unrelated prose would satisfy the check without the instructional content actually being present.
scenarios: A doc edit that mentions 'rebar metrics' only in a cross-reference sentence (not as an invocation instruction) causes AC1's grep to pass even though the Discovery subagent guidance was never actually added.
findings.suggested_fix: AC1: `grep -E 'rebar metrics --since.*--until.*--output json' discovery.md && grep -F 'unavailable' discovery.md`; AC2: `grep -E '^## Metrics' user-guide.md && grep -cE 'agent_process|code_health|delivery|gate_economics' user-guide.md | awk '$1==4'`; AC3: `grep -iE 'metrics.*docs/' AGENTS.md`; AC4: `grep -F 'rebar.metrics' docs/reuse-surface.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the preferred default for verification test placement (e.g. 'prefer a new `tests/scripts/test_metrics_docs.py` grep script unless the existing drift-check infra already covers it') so the executor need not choose.
evidence: 'These can be a lightweight tests/scripts/-tier grep test or folded into the existing docs-index/README drift checks' — the 'or' is a decision point, not a default.
No further guidance narrows the choice.
findings.finding: The Tests section states the verification tests may be 'a lightweight tests/scripts/-tier grep test OR folded into the existing docs-index/README drift checks' without stating which approach is the default — an executor must make a design choice that the plan defers to them.
findings.location: Tests section: 'These can be a lightweight tests/scripts/-tier grep test or folded into the existing docs-index/README drift checks'
scenarios: One executor adds a new test script; another folds into existing checks; both satisfy the plan; but they produce different repo artifacts and may conflict with CI structure expectations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit failure-path guidance in `discovery.md`: specify that if `rebar metrics` exits non-zero or returns unparseable JSON, the agent should log the error, skip the metrics evidence step, and note in its findings that metrics were unavailable due to a command error (mirroring the `unavailable` caveat already required).
evidence: Plan says to note the `unavailable` state so the agent does not treat absence as zero, but gives no guidance for non-zero exit codes, JSON parse errors, or unexpected output shapes.
The conditional 'when the repo is a rebar store' guards the invocation but does not describe the failure handling if the command itself errors after invocation.
AC only asserts presence of `rebar metrics` reference and `unavailable`-state caveat — error/failure path is not in scope of any acceptance criterion.
findings.finding: The discovery.md skill guidance does not specify what the agent should do when `rebar metrics` exits with an error, is not found (repo is a rebar store but command fails), or returns malformed/unexpected JSON — only the `unavailable` (no-signal) state is handled, not command-level failures.
scenarios: Agent invokes `rebar metrics` and the command exits non-zero (e.g., store corruption, missing index); agent may stall, retry infinitely, or treat the failure as a zero-signal result.
JSON output is truncated or schema-mismatched (e.g., future version change); agent may hallucinate metric values or crash its parsing step.
Repo is detected as a rebar store but the `rebar` binary is unavailable in the agent's PATH; no fallback behavior is specified.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `discovery.md` skill instructions, after the `rebar metrics` invocation block, add: "If the command exits non-zero or the output cannot be parsed as valid JSON, skip this evidence source and record: `rebar metrics: command error — skipped` in the Discovery findings. Do not treat a command failure as a zero or unavailable metric value."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a command-level fallback to the `discovery.md` guidance specifying what the subagent should do if `rebar metrics` exits non-zero (e.g., log the error and continue without citing metrics values, noting that `rebar metrics` was unavailable in the evidence).
evidence: Plan Files section only addresses the `unavailable` state: 'Note the `unavailable` state (a metric whose signal has not accrued yet) so the agent does not treat absence as zero'

src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py: exits with code 2 on bad args; `config.repo_root()` can silently fall back to cwd, and individual metric compute callables (e.g., git_metrics._git) call subprocess with `check=True` which raises on git failure
No plan text specifies what the discovery subagent should do if `rebar metrics` returns a non-zero exit code or produces no output
findings.finding: The plan specifies how the subagent should handle the `unavailable` metric state (treat it as 'signal not yet accrued, not as zero') but provides no fallback decision rule for the case where `rebar metrics` itself fails — exits non-zero, command not found, or raises a subprocess error during git-derivation compute. The `_commands/metrics.py` fault-isolates individual evaluate calls, but the command can still fail at the config/store-root level.
scenarios: rebar is not installed in the analysis environment — command not found, agent halts or misinterprets
The repo has .rebar/.env-id but git history is shallow/unavailable — subprocess raises, command exits non-zero, agent has no guidance on whether to skip or report the failure as a finding

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py in AC5 and the Tests section: either extend it to introspect the rebar.metrics callables documented in reuse-surface.md, or explicitly state that the new section documents no callable signatures (pure prose only) and therefore no extension is needed.
evidence: tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py line 1: 'Anti-drift check for the reusable-machinery reference (docs/reuse-surface.md). The doc documents exact signatures of the reuse surface … this fails — turning a manual-inspection claim into a CI-enforced invariant.'
Plan AC5: 'the docs-index / README drift gates still pass (no stale generated docs) after the edits' — no mention of test_reuse_surface_doc.py
Plan Tests section lists only 'a lightweight tests/scripts/-tier grep test or folded into the existing docs-index/README drift checks' — omits the existing reuse-surface anti-drift gate
findings.finding: The plan's AC5 ('the docs-index / README drift gates still pass') omits the pre-existing tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py anti-drift gate, which introspects every documented callable in docs/reuse-surface.md and asserts its real signature matches the doc. If the new rebar.metrics section in reuse-surface.md documents any callable signatures (e.g. is_authoritative, MetricSpec fields, evaluate), this test must be extended — but the plan neither mentions it nor includes it in the test deliverables.
findings.location: AC5 / Tests section / docs/reuse-surface.md edit target
scenarios: Author adds a rebar.metrics section to reuse-surface.md that documents MetricSpec or is_authoritative signatures → test_reuse_surface_doc.py fails in CI because the new section's callables are not covered by the introspection test
Author adds the section without documenting callables (pure prose) → test passes but docs/reuse-surface.md precedent is broken (every other subsystem section documents exact signatures)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Tests section, add: 'If the reuse-surface.md metrics section documents any callable signatures (e.g. is_authoritative, MetricSpec fields), extend tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py with a matching introspection assertion — otherwise CI will fail on the existing anti-drift gate.' Update AC5 to include: 'and tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py still passes'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py` (or add a companion test) to cover the `rebar.metrics` symbols documented in the new `reuse-surface.md` section, so the existing 'verified against current source' promise holds for the new content too.
evidence: Plan Tests section lists only grep-based checks: 'that docs/reuse-surface.md references rebar.metrics' — no mention of extending test_reuse_surface_doc.py

reuse-surface.md line 17-19: '> Every signature below is verified against the current source by `tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py` (a CI anti-drift gate that introspects each documented callable) — re-verify with `inspect.signature(...)` if in doubt.'
test_reuse_surface_doc.py enumerates only the four existing subsystems (signing, runner, prompts, output-schema); rebar.metrics symbols are not tested there

Plan Approach section: 'reference the reusable `rebar.metrics` subsystem (the `MetricSpec`/`REGISTRY` declarative registry + `is_authoritative` segregation helper)' — these are documentable callables/objects
findings.finding: The plan adds a new `rebar.metrics` subsystem section to `docs/reuse-surface.md` that will document `MetricSpec`/`REGISTRY`/`is_authoritative` — but does not plan to extend `tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py` to cover those symbols. The existing doc carries an explicit promise ('Every signature below is verified against the current source by `tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py`') that would become misleading or false for the new section, and the new content would have no anti-drift protection, undermining the discoverability durability the plan aims to achieve.
scenarios: An implementer documents `MetricSpec` fields or `is_authoritative` signature in reuse-surface.md; a later change renames a field or parameter; the anti-drift test does not catch it because test_reuse_surface_doc.py was never extended; the doc silently drifts.

A consumer reads reuse-surface.md, trusts the 'verified against current source' promise, uses the documented `MetricSpec` constructor arguments, and gets a runtime error because the signature drifted.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test function in `test_reuse_surface_doc.py` (e.g. `test_metrics_surface_signatures_match_doc`) that introspects `rebar.metrics.is_authoritative`, `rebar.metrics.evaluate`, and `rebar.metrics.MetricSpec` fields — mirroring the pattern of existing signature checks — and include this in the plan's Tests section and AC list.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'complexity/clone/coverage trends' are snapshot-backed metrics that rebar metrics will surface (stating they appear as 'unavailable' until backfilled). No complexity, clone, or coverage metric is registered in the REGISTRY anywhere in src/rebar/metrics/. The github_actions.py coverage adapter explicitly registers nothing into REGISTRY. The planned janitor skill instruction — and the user-guide section — would direct agents and users to look for metric values that do not exist in any form.
findings.location: ## Why (paragraph 2: 'snapshot-backed complexity/clone/coverage trends'); ## Files → examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md ('complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Why section and the Files/discovery.md guidance, remove all references to 'complexity/clone/coverage' as rebar metrics values. The plan should confine the janitor's `rebar metrics` citation to the metrics that actually exist: module-size distribution (module_size_distribution / module_size_trend / oversized_module_count), churn, refactor_to_addition_ratio, cap_change_events (code_health lens), the agent_process metrics (attempts_per_ticket, rework_within_7_days, revert_recovery, reopen_recovery, first_pass_rate), and gate_economics metrics.

findings.finding: The discovery.md skill guidance (Files section) instructs agents that 'complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled,' but no complexity, clone, or coverage MetricSpecs exist in the actual REGISTRY (registry.py, git_metrics.py, event_metrics.py, sidecar_metrics.py were all checked). These metric IDs will not appear in `rebar metrics --output json` output at all — not even as `unavailable` entries — so an agent following the skill will search for fields that are simply absent, producing misplaced effort or confusion.
findings.location: Files section → examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md description; also the Why section ('complexity/clone/coverage trends where those snapshots have accrued')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled' with 'additional metrics (e.g. complexity, clone, coverage) are not yet registered; omit them from evidence rather than treating absence as zero.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'complexity/clone/coverage trends where those snapshots have accrued (otherwise `unavailable`)' are part of the `rebar metrics` output, implying these metrics exist in the REGISTRY but report `unavailable` until backfilled. The actual REGISTRY (registry.py + all three reader-module registrations: event_metrics.py, git_metrics.py, sidecar_metrics.py) has no complexity, clone, or coverage metric specs at all — they would not appear in `rebar metrics` output even as `unavailable`. A janitor agent following the skill guidance and looking for these entries in JSON output will find them absent (not `unavailable`), undermining the cited-evidence model.
findings.location: Files section (discovery.md description) and Why section: 'complexity/clone/coverage trends where those snapshots have accrued (otherwise `unavailable`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove 'complexity/clone/coverage' from both the discovery.md guidance and the user-guide Metrics section description (or replace with a future-tense caveat such as 'complexity/clone/coverage specs are planned but not yet registered; they will not appear in current output'). The four confirmed code-health metrics (module_size_distribution, oversized_module_count, churn, refactor_to_addition_ratio) plus the delivery and gate_economics lenses are sufficient evidence for the janitor use-case.

findings.finding: The plan instructs the rebar-janitor discovery skill to cite 'complexity/clone/coverage' metrics from `rebar metrics` output — but no complexity, clone, or coverage metrics exist anywhere in the REGISTRY. The instruction will cause the discovery subagent to look for metric IDs that are simply absent from the JSON output, creating precisely the 'treat absence as zero' failure mode the plan warns against.
findings.location: Files section (discovery.md description): 'complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled'; also Why section: 'snapshot-backed complexity/clone/coverage trends where those snapshots have accrued'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files/discovery.md description and Why section, replace 'complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled' with the actual registered code_health metrics: module_size_distribution (distribution dict), oversized_module_count (integer), churn (insertions/deletions dict), refactor_to_addition_ratio (float or unavailable). These are the real durable signals the skill should cite.

findings.finding: The plan's `discovery.md` guidance asserts that 'complexity/clone/coverage are snapshot-backed and may read `unavailable` until backfilled,' implying an agent will see `unavailable` entries for those IDs in `rebar metrics --output json`. But no MetricSpec with IDs for complexity, clone, or coverage is registered in the actual REGISTRY (verified across all three registration modules: event_metrics.py, git_metrics.py, sidecar_metrics.py, and the seed specs in registry.py). Unregistered metrics produce no JSON output at all — not even an `unavailable` entry — so an agent following this guidance will find nothing where it expects something.
findings.location: Files section — `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md` guidance; also implicit in the 'Why' section ('snapshot-backed complexity/clone/coverage trends where those snapshots have accrued')
findings.suggested_fix: In discovery.md and the Why section, replace the complexity/clone/coverage claim with the actual registered metric IDs that currently produce output (module_size_distribution, oversized_module_count, churn, refactor_to_addition_ratio for code_health; agent_process and gate_economics IDs for the other lenses). If complexity/clone/coverage are genuinely planned for future registration, frame them as 'not yet registered' rather than 'snapshot-backed and `unavailable`' — because `unavailable` implies the metric ID appears in the JSON output, which it will not until the MetricSpec is added to REGISTRY.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete proving command for AC5's drift gates (e.g. `make docs-check` or `pytest tests/scripts/test_docs_index.py`) so the criterion is verifiable without investigation.
evidence: 'the docs-index / README drift gates still pass (no stale generated docs) after the edits'
No command, script path, or CI job name is given for these gates anywhere in the plan.
ABSENCE: no 'make docs-check' or equivalent invocation is mentioned.
findings.finding: AC5 references 'docs-index / README drift gates' as the proving check but never names the command or script that runs them; if those gates are not already discoverable in the repo, an executing agent cannot verify this criterion without investigation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'the docs-index / README drift gates still pass'
scenarios: An agent completing the edits has no way to deterministically verify AC5 without first discovering what command drives the drift gate — requiring investigation the plan defers to the executor.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the docs-index / README drift gates still pass' with 'run `<exact command>` (e.g. `make docs-check`) and assert exit 0 — no stale generated docs after the edits'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief description of what the 'docs-index / README drift gates' are (e.g., a named script/CI check) and whether any action is needed to keep them passing after the four file edits, or remove AC5 if the gates are guaranteed unaffected by purely additive section additions.
evidence: AC5 text: 'the docs-index / README drift gates still pass (no stale generated docs) after the edits'
Tests section describes grep checks only against the four named files; no mention of a docs-index or README drift check mechanism
Files section lists exactly four files; none is a docs-index or README
Approach section does not describe any action related to drift gates
findings.finding: AC5 ('the docs-index / README drift gates still pass after the edits') verifies a gate whose existence and mechanics are never described anywhere in the plan body — no Files entry, no Approach step, and no Tests description explains what these gates are or what (if anything) the implementer must do to keep them passing. It is an orphan criterion: it references an entity not defined or described in the plan.
scenarios: An implementer makes the four file edits and then has no idea whether AC5 requires them to run a script, update a manifest, or do nothing — they cannot verify the criterion without external knowledge not provided by the plan.
If the drift gate does trigger on user-guide.md additions (e.g., a TOC or index file must be regenerated), the criterion would fail silently and the plan gives no remediation path.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a sentence in the Approach or Tests section naming the drift-gate mechanism (e.g., 'the existing `tests/scripts/check-docs-index.sh` grep gate — no update needed because we are adding sections to existing files, not creating new files') so AC5 is self-contained, or (b) remove AC5 if it adds no actionable verification value for this purely additive change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the 'do not cite complexity/clone/coverage from `rebar metrics`' constraint from the Why section into the Files section's `discovery.md` specification (the text that will actually be written into the skill file), so it is co-located with the guidance the subagent reads.
evidence: Why section: '(Complexity/clone/coverage snapshots exist in the store but are not yet surfaced by a registered metric, so they do not appear in `rebar metrics` output — do not cite them.)'

Files section for discovery.md: lists what to cite (module-size distribution, oversized-module count, churn, refactor-to-addition ratio, cap-change events) but does not include the 'do not cite complexity/clone/coverage' constraint

The rebar-janitor SKILL.md already mentions 'Clone / duplication trend' and 'Refactor ratio' in the temporal decay pass — a subagent reading discovery.md could conflate git-derived clone/churn signals with `rebar metrics` clone/coverage metrics unless the constraint is co-located
findings.finding: The plan's Why section contains a pink-elephant corrective instruction — 'do not cite [complexity/clone/coverage]' — that is not co-located with the `discovery.md` file text the plan specifies writing. The prohibition lives only in the Why section (a plan-authoring note), not in the Files description for `discovery.md` or in AC1. An executor writing `discovery.md` may not carry this constraint forward into the actual skill file.
findings.location: Why section: 'Complexity/clone/coverage snapshots exist in the store but are not yet surfaced by a registered metric... do not cite them'; Files section for `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/phases/discovery.md`
scenarios: Executor writes discovery.md following only the Files section spec and omits the complexity/clone/coverage exclusion
A future subagent reads the updated discovery.md and, seeing no exclusion, attempts to cite complexity metrics from `rebar metrics` output — finds them absent, treats absence as zero
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section for discovery.md, add: 'Note: complexity, clone, and coverage metrics are NOT registered in `rebar metrics` output — do not cite them from this command; use the separate temporal-decay git pass for those signals.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
